, 3: The sklearn Workflow
At a high level, there are three steps to creating an sklearn model:
Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
1
Initialize a new model instance
Make a "copy" of the model template
my_model = 1m. LinearRegression()
2
Fit the model to the training data
Save the optimal model parameters
my_model.fit(X, y)
3
Use fitted model to make predictions
Fitted model makes predictions
my_model.predict(X_new)
To extract the fitted parameters: my_model. coef_and my_model.intercept_

Audio: So we have the body mass of the penguin. The slippery length of penguin, I was trying to predict the build-ups. These blue dots are my data. And that plane... Is my mother? Do you do a good job? What's that, right? I had to enter if I did not have an intercept term. What would be true about this plane? It goes to the origin, very good. Why would it go to the origin? Because when I say that... Body Master Zero, Flip Related Zero Zero times state of one plus zero times state of two and there's no intercept term is Zero. So the thing I can predict when I have body mass zero flip math or flipper length zero is a build up task to be zero. But I'm in your set term so now build up can be that intercept term when there's zero zero. So it's no longer in city version. And I didn't plot the origin, but you could extend this plot to see that this plane does not pass the origin. Back, where does it go through at the origin? 11. We know that the plane at the origin is going to be 11. Not here. Because we say when everything else is zero, the intercept term is 11. Video: 1: File

Manually Descending the Gradient
Armed with this knowledge, let's try to see if we can use the derivative to optimize the function.
We start by making some guess for the minimizing value of x. Then, we look at the derivative of the function at this
value of x, and step downhill in the opposite direction. We can express our new rule as a recurrence relation:
x (+1) = x(*)
darf()
We obtain our next guess for the minimizing value of x at timestep t + 1 (x(t+1)) by taking the guess our last
guess (x(*) and subtracting the derivative of the function at that point ( d. f(x(*).

Manually Descending the Gradient
Armed with this knowledge, let's try to see if we can use the derivative to optimize the function.
We start by making some guess for the minimizing value of x. Then, we look at the derivative of the function at this
value of x, and step downhill in the opposite direction. We can express our new rule as a recurrence relation:
x (+1) = x(*)
darf()
We obtain our next guess for the minimizing value of x at timestep t + 1 (x(t+1)) by taking the guess our last
guess (x(*) and subtracting the derivative of the function at that point ( d. f(x(*).

[ ]:
plot_steps (steps)
Looking pretty good! We do have a problem though - once we arrive close to the minimum value of the function, our
guesses "bounce" back and forth past the minimum without ever reaching it.
In other words, each step we take when updating our guess moves us too far. We can address this by decreasing the
size of each step.
Let's update our algorithm to use a learning rate (also sometimes called the step size), which controls how far we
move with each update. We represent the learning rate with a.

Looking pretty good! We do have a problem though - once we arrive close to the minimum value of the function, our
guesses "bounce" back and forth past the minimum without ever reaching it.
In other words, each step we take when updating our guess moves us too far. We can address this by decreasing the
size of each step.
Let's update our algorithm to use a learning rate (also sometimes called the step size), which controls how far we
move with each update. We represent the learning rate with a.
a(+1) = x(*) - a dar f(a)(f)
A small & means that we will take small update steps; a large @ means we will take large steps.
Let's update our function to use a = 0.3.

value of x, and step downhill in the opposite direction. We can express our new rule as a recurrence relation:
a (+1) = x(t) - de f(az(t)
+
We obtain our next guess for the minimizing value of x at timestep t + 1 (x(+1)) by taking the guess our last
guess (x(*) and subtracting the derivative of the function at that point (dr f(x(+).

Running the algorithm one step at a time

Manually Descending the Gradient
Armed with this knowledge, let's try to see if we can use the derivative to optimize the function.
We start by making some guess for the minimizing value of x. Then, we look at the derivative of the function at this
value of x, and step downhill in the opposite direction. We can express our new rule as a recurrence relation:
2 (+1) = x(*) - a. f(ac (t)
We obtain our next guess for the minimizing value of x at timestep t + 1 (x(+1)) by taking the guess our last
guess (x(*) and subtracting the derivative of the function at that point ( dr. f(x(*).

def gradient_descent(df, initial_guess, alpha, n):
"" "Performs n steps of gradient descent on df using learning rate alpha starting
from initial_guess. Returns a numpy array of all guesses over time. """
guesses = [initial_guess ]
current_guess = initial_guess
while len(guesses) < n:
current_guess = current_guess - alpha * df(current_guess)
guesses . append (current_guess)
return np. array (guesses)

def gradient_descent(df, initial_guess, alpha, n):
"" "Performs n steps of gradient descent on df using learning rate alpha starting
from initial_guess. Returns a numpy array of all guesses over time. """
guesses = [initial_guess ]
current_guess = initial_guess
while len(guesses) < n:
current_guess = current_guess - alpha * df(current_guess)
guesses . append (current_guess)
return np. array (guesses)

The Gradient Descent Algorithm
The process we just explored above is called gradient descent - we compute the derivative of a function (its gradient) with respect to
the variable we wish to optimize, then descend down to the minimum of the function.
2(+1)= = (6) - a dar f(x)
In the cell below, we define gradient_descent , which runs the gradient descent algorithm for a specified number n of updates and
stores all guesses.
[ ]: def gradient_descent(grad_f, initial_guess, alpha, n):
"""Performs n steps of gradient descent on df using learning rate alpha starting
from initial_guess. Returns a numpy array of all guesses over time. """
guesses = [initial_guess ]
current_guess = initial_guess
while len(guesses) < n:
current_guess = current_guess - alpha * grad_f(current_guess)
guesses. append (current_guess)
return np. array (guesses)
Below, we see a visualization of the trajectory taken by this algorithm. Try modifying the initial_guess , learning rate alpha , and
number of updates n .

def gradient_descent(df, initial_guess, alpha, n):
"" "Performs n steps of gradient descent on df using learning rate alpha starting
from initial_guess. Returns a numpy array of all guesses over time. """
guesses = [initial_guess ]
current_guess = initial_guess
while len(guesses) < n:
current_guess = current_guess - alpha * df(current_guess)
guesses . append (current_guess)
return np. array (guesses)

The Gradient Descent Algorithm
The process we just explored above is called gradient descent - we compute the derivative of a function (its gradient) with respect to
the variable we wish to optimize, then descend down to the minimum of the function.
2(+1)= = (6) - a dar f(x)
In the cell below, we define gradient_descent , which runs the gradient descent algorithm for a specified number n of updates and
stores all guesses.
[ ]: def gradient_descent(grad_f, initial_guess, alpha, n):
"""Performs n steps of gradient descent on df using learning rate alpha starting
from initial_guess. Returns a numpy array of all guesses over time. """
guesses = [initial_guess ]
current_guess = initial_guess
while len(guesses) < n:
current_guess = current_guess - alpha * grad_f(current_guess)
guesses. append (current_guess)
return np. array (guesses)
Below, we see a visualization of the trajectory taken by this algorithm. Try modifying the initial_guess , learning rate alpha , and
number of updates n .

This basic approach suffers from three major flaws:
1. If the minimum is outside our range of guesses, the answer will be completely wrong.
2. Even if our range of guesses is correct, if the guesses are too coarse, our answer will be inaccurate.
3. It is not computationally efficient, considering potentially vast numbers of bad guesses

def gradient_descent (grad_f, initial_guess, alpha, h):
"""Performs n steps of gradient descent on df using learning rate alpha starting
from initial_guess. Returns a numpy array of all guesses over time."""
guesses = [initial_guess]
current_guess = initial_guess
while len(guesses) < n:
current_guess = current_guess - alpha * grad_f (current_guess)
guesses. append ( current_guess)
return np. array (guesses)

number of updates n .

def gradient_descent(df, initial_guess, alpha, n):
"" "Performs n steps of gradient descent on df using learning rate alpha starting
from initial_guess. Returns a numpy array of all guesses over time. """
guesses = [initial_guess]
current_guess = initial_guess
while len(guesses) < n:
current_guess = current_guess - alpha * df(current_guess)
guesses . append (current_guess)
return np. array (guesses)

def gradient_descent(df, initial_guess, alpha, n):
"" "Performs n steps of gradient descent on df using learning rate alpha starting
from initial_guess. Returns a numpy array of all guesses over time. """
guesses = [initial_guess]
current_guess = initial_guess
while len(guesses) < n:
current_guess = current_guess - alpha * df(current_guess)
guesses . append (current_guess)
return np. array (guesses)
